title: Film & video: one work, many physical elements
intro: A film exists once intellectually but in many physical elements — camera negative, viewing print, digital master. The key RiC move is to keep the work as one Record and model each element as an Instantiation, so they're comparable and none is mistaken for a separate film. This scenario branches single-film vs collection, attaches the makers, then captures the elements.
question: Is a film a Record, and the negative, print and digital master all Instantiations of it? Who are the creators?

steps.prompt: One film, or a collection of films?

choices.label: A single film
choices.why: Good — one film is one Record; its physical elements are Instantiations of it.

choices.label: A film collection
choices.why: Good — a collection (a director's output, a studio's library) is a Record Set, each film a member Record.

choices.label: An Instantiation
choices.why: Not the starting point — an Instantiation is a physical element (a print, a file). First choose the intellectual unit.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The film as a work is one Record. The negative, the print and the digital master are all Instantiations of it.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one film — a Set is for a collection of films.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's one physical element, not the work. Keep the film as a Record and add elements as Instantiations.

capture.comment: Create the film as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The collection aggregates many films — a Record Set — each a member Record with its own elements.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — a collection is many films. Use a Record Set.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit — a collection is an aggregation, not a component. (A reel within one film could be a Record Part.)

capture.comment: Create the collection as a Record Set.

steps.prompt: Attach a maker (director or production company).

choices.label: Agent + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. Directors are People and studios are Corporate Bodies — both Agents. Relate the film to each with has_creator; a film commonly has several. Reuse each Agent across their whole filmography.

choices.label: Credits as a text blob
choices.why: Doesn't fit for description. The full credits can be a note, but the key makers should be reconcilable Agents so their work is findable.

capture.comment: Create the production company as an Agent.
Link the film to the maker with has_creator (repeat for the director, etc.).

steps.prompt: Now the physical elements: the 16mm camera negative, a viewing print, and a digital preservation master.

choices.label: Three Instantiations of the one film
choices.why: Fits. Each element is an Instantiation of the film Record, linked with has_instantiation — each with its own gauge, base, condition and fixity. That's how an archive tracks 'which elements survive' for a single work.

choices.label: Three separate Records
choices.why: Doesn't fit. They aren't three films — they're three carriers of one film. Model them as Instantiations of the one Record.

choices.label: Notes on the film
choices.why: Too flat. Elements have distinct formats, condition and provenance and must be tracked individually — Instantiations.

capture.comment: Create the camera negative as an Instantiation (repeat for print + digital master).
Link the film to the element with has_instantiation.

outcome.verdict: One work, its makers, and every surviving element.
outcome.summary: The film is a Record (or the collection a Record Set); directors and studios are reusable Agents; and the negative, print and digital master are Instantiations of the one Record. You can now answer 'which elements of this film survive, and in what condition' — and find everything a studio produced.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
